text: This is a technical guide on robotic external iliac deep inguinal and obturator lymph node dissection.

This is a forty-five years old male with stage three anorectal melanoma with right inguinal lymph node metastasis. His initial symptoms include tinnitus and anal pain for a few months. After his staging scans, he then went on to have neoadjuvant immunotherapy consisting of nivolumab and ipilimumab. On the third cycle of immunotherapy, he had concurrent focal anorectal radiotherapy as well.

From the initial evaluation using flexible endoscopy, you can tell that this patient has an extensive disease involving the exterior of the anal canal, extending throughout the anal canal and into his rectum. This was further confirmed by the MRI of the pelvis, which showed that this tumor extended approximately six centimeters from the anorectal junction.
After his neoadjuvant treatment, we restaged his disease, and the CT showed a persistent superficial inguinal nerve node and suspicious external iliac nerve nodes.

Therefore, the operative steps were robotic abdominal perineal resection, the robot was redocked again to perform the external iliac, deep inguinal and obturator lymph node dissection. As for the positioning, the patient was positioned in a tridimervous position with access to the right groin and the pictogram on the right side shows the proposed placement for the robotic approach.

The surgeon identifies the ureter, obturator, umbilical artery, and vas deferens, beginning with an incision in the peritoneum over the external iliac vessels. The vas deferens is exposed first, followed by lateral incision near the obliterated umbilical artery. Sharp dissection of areolar tissue near the ureter is performed to access deeper layers. Blunt and sharp dissection exposes the deep portion of the obliterated umbilical artery, and the vas deferens is further exposed. The peritoneum is incised along the obliterated umbilical artery, providing a clear view for external iliac nerve node dissection, including the deep inguinal ring and obturator lymph node packet.

The surgeon dissects down to the adventitia of the external iliac artery, incising the areolar tissue to expose the proximal part of the artery. The dissection continues towards the deep inguinal ring, preserving a nerve structure by pushing it away from the lymph node packet near the psoas muscle.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lymph node packet from the external iliac artery, identifying and cauterizing an overlying vessel. The proximal part of the lymph node packet is skeletonized, and a feeding vessel is secured with a hemi-lock before being excised using CoTRI. The dissection continues under the external iliac artery, allowing the lymph node packet to be mobilized to the opposite side.

The surgeon performs a deep inguinal lymph node dissection, carefully dissecting the lymph node packet off the distal portion of the external iliac artery. Using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, the surgeon progresses towards the deep inguinal ring, aiming to connect with the previously established dissection plane from the superficial inguinal lymph node dissection.

The surgeon identifies the most distal extent of the dissection by locating a clip placed during the open procedure. The dissection proceeds medially, removing the lymph node packet from the external iliac vein. As the dissection approaches the deep inguinal ring, a small vein situated above the external iliac vein is recognized, marking the extent of the dissection above the vein.

The surgeon continues the dissection into the deeper portion, carefully removing the external iliac vein. The muscle belly becomes visible as the dissection proceeds under a small vein. The areolar tissue is dissected to reveal the clip placed during the open procedure.

The surgeon performs an obturator lymph node dissection, starting by dissecting the lymph node packet along the external iliac vein. The thin external iliac vein requires adequate counter traction to expose the areolar tissue, which is sharply incised using cautery. The dissection proceeds from medial to lateral around the obturator muscle, carefully separating the lymph node packet to identify the obturator nerve and artery. The obturator artery is exposed medial to the nerve. The surgeon then dissects the lymph node packet off the internal iliac artery, using blunt dissection near the obturator nerve to avoid stimulation, completing the obturator dissection.

The final histopathology showed an anorectal melanoma with extensive tumor regression and twenty-two negative lymph nodes. The only positive lymph nodes was identified in the superficial inguinal lymph node region, where there is one out of twelve. The external iliac, the inguinal and obturator lymph nodes had fifty negative lymph nodes, but there were aggregates of melanophages with non-viable tumors identified. That concludes our technical guide.
